op.name: sqrt
op.summary: Return the non-negative square-root of an array, element-wise.
op.signature: we.sqrt(x, /, out=None, *, where=True, casting='same_kind', order='K', dtype=None, subok=True[, signature])

blocks.text: Return the non-negative square-root of an array, element-wise.

desc_blocks.text: The values whose square-roots are required.

items.name: out
items.type: ndarray, None, or tuple of ndarray and None, optional

desc_blocks.text:
A location into which the result is stored. If provided, it must have
a shape that the inputs broadcast to. If not provided or None,
a freshly-allocated array is returned. A tuple (possible only as a
keyword argument) must have length equal to the number of outputs.

This condition is broadcast over the input. At locations where the
condition is True, the `out` array will be set to the ufunc result.
Elsewhere, the `out` array will retain its original value.
Note that if an uninitialized `out` array is created via the default
``out=None``, locations within it where the condition is False will
remain uninitialized.

For other keyword-only arguments, see the
:ref:`ufunc docs <ufuncs.kwargs>`.

An array of the same shape as `x`, containing the positive
square-root of each element in `x`.  If any element in `x` is
complex, a complex array is returned (and the square-roots of
negative reals are calculated).  If all of the elements in `x`
are real, so is `y`, with negative elements returning ``nan``.
If `out` was provided, `y` is a reference to it.
This is a scalar if `x` is a scalar.

blocks.text:
*sqrt* has--consistent with common convention--as its branch cut the
real "interval" [`-inf`, 0), and is continuous from above on it.
A branch cut is a curve in the complex plane across which a given
complex function fails to be continuous.

blocks.code:
>>> import numpy as np
>>> np.sqrt([1,4,9])
array([ 1.,  2.,  3.])

blocks.text: emath.sqrt — A version which returns complex numbers when given negative reals. Note that 0.0 and -0.0 are handled differently for complex inputs.
